options.label: 15 to 19 years old
options.description: Deduct 5 points among STR and SIZ. Deduct 5 points from EDU. Roll twice to generate a Luck score (see Luck, above) and use the higher value.

options.label: 20 to 39 years old
options.description: Make an improvement check for EDU.

options.label: 40 to 49 years old
options.description: Make 2 improvement checks for EDU and deduct 5 points among STR, CON or DEX, and reduce APP by 5.

options.label: 50 to 59 years old
options.description: Make 3 improvement checks for EDU and deduct 10 points among STR, CON or DEX, and reduce APP by 10.

options.label: 60 to 69 years old
options.description: Make 4 improvement checks for EDU and deduct 20 points among STR, CON or DEX, and reduce APP by 15.

options.label: 70 to 79 years old
options.description: Make 4 improvement checks for EDU and deduct 40 points among STR, CON or DEX, and reduce APP by 20.

options.label: 80 to 89 years old
options.description: Make 4 improvement checks for EDU and deduct 80 points among STR, CON or DEX, and reduce APP by 25.

options.label: Accountant
options.description: Either employed within a business or working as a freelance consultant with a portfolio of self-employed clients or businesses. Diligence and an attention to detail means that most accountants can make good researchers, being able to support investigations through the careful analysis of personal and business transactions, financial statements, and other records.
options.suggestedContacts: Business associates, legal professions, financial sector (bankers, other accountants).

options.label: Acrobat
options.description: Acrobats may be either amateur-athletes competing in staged meets—possibly even the Olympics—or professionals employed with the entertainment sector (e.g. circuses, carnivals, theatrical performances).
options.suggestedContacts: Amateur athletic circles, sports writers, circuses, carnivals.

options.label: Stage Actor
options.description: Usually a stage or film actor. Many stage actors have a background in the classics and, considering themselves legitimate, have a tendency to look down upon the commercial efforts of the film industry.
options.suggestedContacts: Theatre industry, newspaper arts critics, actor's guild or union.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Film Star
options.description: Movie stars and the film industry have long captured the interest of people across the world. Many stars are made overnight and most of them lead flashy, high profile lives, ever in the media spotlight.
options.suggestedContacts: Film industry, media critics, writers.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Agency Detective
options.description: Company Operatives are plainclothes detectives, sent out on cases requiring them to solve mysteries, prevent murders, locate missing people, and so on.
options.suggestedContacts: Local law enforcement, clients.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Alienist [Classic]
options.description: In the 1920s, alienist is the term given for those who treat mental illness (early psychiatrists). Psychoanalysis is barely known in the U.S., and its basis in sexual life and toilet training is felt to be indecent. Psychiatry, a standard medical education augmented by behaviorism, is more common.
options.suggestedContacts: Others in the field of mental illness, medical doctors, and occasionally detectives in law enforcement.

options.label: Animal Trainer
options.description: May be employed by film studios, a traveling circus, a horse stable, or possibly working freelance. Whether training guide dogs for the blind or teaching a lion to jump through a flaming hoop, the animal trainer usually works alone, spending long hours in close proximity with the animals in their care.
options.suggestedContacts: Zoos, circus folk, patrons, actors.

options.label: Antiquarian [Lovecraftian]
options.description: A person who delights in the timeless excellence of design and execution, and in the power of ancient lore. Probably the most Lovecraft-like occupation available to an investigator. An independent income allows the antiquarian to explore things old and obscure.
options.suggestedContacts: Booksellers, antique collectors, historical societies.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Antique Dealer
options.description: Antique dealers usually own their own shop, retail items out of their homes, or go on extended buying trips, making a profit on reselling to urban stores.
options.suggestedContacts: Local historians, other antique dealers, possibly criminal fences.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Archaeologist [Lovecraftian]
options.description: The study and exploration of the past. Primarily the identification, examination, and analysis of recovered materials relating to human history. The work involves painstaking research and meticulous study, not to mention a willing attitude to getting one's hands dirty.
options.suggestedContacts: Patrons, museums, universities.
skills: Navigate or Science (e.g. chemistry, physics, geology)

options.label: Architect
options.description: Architects are trained to design and plan buildings, whether a small conversion to a private house or a multi-million dollar construction project. Architects must be aware of local planning laws, health and safety regulation, and general public safety.
options.suggestedContacts: Local building and city engineering departments, construction firms.

options.label: Artist
options.description: May be a painter, sculptor, etc. Sometimes self-absorbed and driven with a particular vision, sometimes blessed with a great talent that is able to inspire passion and understanding.
options.suggestedContacts: Art galleries, critics, wealthy patrons, the advertising industry.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Asylum Attendant
options.description: Although there are private sanitariums for those few who can afford them, the vast bulk of the mentally ill are housed in state and county facilities. They employ a large number of attendants, often chosen more for their strength and size rather than medical learning.
options.suggestedContacts: Medical staff, patients, and relatives of patients. Access to medical records, as well as drugs and other medical supplies.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Athlete
options.description: Probably plays in a professional baseball, football, cricket, or basketball team. Successful professional athletes will enjoy a certain amount of celebrity within the arena of their expertise.
options.suggestedContacts: Sports personalities, sports writers, other media stars.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Author [Lovecraftian]
options.description: As distinct from the journalist, the author uses words to define and explore the human condition, especially the range of human emotions. Their labors are solitary and the rewards solipsistic: only a relative handful make much money in the present day.
options.suggestedContacts: Publishers, critics, historians, etc.

options.label: Bartender
options.description: Normally not the owner of the bar, the bartender is everyone's friend. For some it's a career or their business, for many it's a means to an end. In the 1920s the profession is made illegal by the Prohibition Act; however, there's no shortage of work for a bartender, as someone has to serve the drinks in the speakeasies and secret gin joints.
options.suggestedContacts: Regular customers, possibly organized crime.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Big Game Hunter
options.description: Big game hunters are skilled trackers and hunters who usually earn their living leading safaris for wealthy clients. Most are specialized in one part of the world, such as the Canadian woods, African plains, and other locales.
options.suggestedContacts: Foreign government officials, game wardens, past (usually wealthy) clients, black-market gangs and traders, zoo owners.

options.label: Book Dealer
options.description: A book dealer may be the owner of a retail outlet or niche mail order service, or specialize in buying trips across the country and even overseas. Many will have wealthy or regular clients, who provide lists of sought-after and rare works.
options.suggestedContacts: Bibliographers, book dealers, libraries and universities, clients.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Bounty Hunter
options.description: Bounty hunters track down and return fugitives to justice. Most often, freelancers are employed by Bail Bondsmen to track down bail jumpers. Bounty hunters may freely cross state lines in pursuit of their quarry.
options.suggestedContacts: Bail bondsmen, local police, criminal informants.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Fast Talk, Charm, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Boxer/Wrestler
options.description: Professional boxers and wrestlers are managed by individuals (promoters) possibly backed by outside interests, and usually locked into contracts. Amateur and post-professional boxers and wrestlers can sometimes be found making a living from illegal bare-knuckle fights.
options.suggestedContacts: Sports promoters, journalists, organized crime, professional trainers.

options.label: Butler/Valet/Maid
options.description: This occupation covers those who are employed in a servant capacity and includes butler, valet, and lady's maid. A butler is usually employed as a domestic servant for a large household. A valet or lady's maid provides personal services.
options.suggestedContacts: Waiting staff of other households, local businesses and household suppliers.

options.label: Clergy, Member of the
options.description: The hierarchy of the Church usually assigns clergy to their respective parishes or sends them on evangelical missions. Many clergy (not just Catholic priests) bear witness to confessions and, though they are not at liberty to divulge such secrets, they are free to act upon them.
options.suggestedContacts: Church hierarchy, local congregations, community leaders.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Computer Programmer/Technician [Modern]
options.description: Usually designing, writing, testing, debugging, and/or maintaining the source code of computer programs, the computer programmer is an expert in many different subjects, including formal logic and application platforms.
options.suggestedContacts: Other IT workers, corporate workers and managers, specialized Internet web communities.

options.label: Hacker [Modern]
options.description: The computer hacker uses computers and computer networks as a means of protest to promote political ends (sometimes referred to as hacktivists) or for criminal gain. Illegally breaking into computers and other user accounts is required.
options.suggestedContacts: IT workers, specialized Internet web communities, political groups, criminal enterprises.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Cowboy/girl
options.description: Cowboys work the ranges and ranches of the West. Some own their own ranches, but many are simply hired where and when work is available. Good money can also be made by those willing to risk life and limb on the rodeo circuit.
options.suggestedContacts: Local businesspeople, state agricultural departments, rodeo promoters, and entertainers.

options.label: Craftsperson
options.description: May be equally termed an artisan or master craftsperson. The craftsperson is essentially skilled in the manual production of items or materials. Possible trades include: furniture, jewelry, watchmaker, potter, blacksmith, textiles, calligraphy, sewing, carpentry, book binding, glassblowing, toy maker, stained glass.
options.suggestedContacts: Local business people, other craftspersons and artists.

options.label: Criminal: Assassin
options.description: Hit men are the cold-blooded killers of the underworld. Usually hired from somewhere out of town, they arrive, do their work, then quickly disappear. It's strictly business.
options.suggestedContacts: Few, mostly underworld; people prefer not to know them too well. The best will have earned a formidable reputation on the street.

options.label: Criminal: Bank Robber
options.description: Criminals who specialize in robbing banks and financial institutions, often working as part of an organized gang.
options.suggestedContacts: Other gang members (current and retired), criminal freelancers, organized crime.

options.label: Criminal: Bootlegger/Thug
options.description: Whether bootleggers, corner-boys, or thugs, these are the soldiers of organized crime. They are supported by criminal organizations and are the ones usually expected to take the fall for the gangsters above them.
options.suggestedContacts: Organized crime, street-level law enforcement, local traders.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Criminal: Burglar
options.description: Criminals who specialize in breaking and entering, taking valuables from homes and businesses.
options.suggestedContacts: Fences, other burglars.

options.label: Criminal: Conman
options.description: Confidence tricksters are usually smooth talkers. Working alone or in a team, they descend on wealthy individuals or communities, fleecing their targets of their hard-earned savings.
options.suggestedContacts: Other confidence artists, freelance criminals.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Criminal: Freelance/Solo
options.description: Criminals who work independently, perhaps occasionally teaming up with others when the reward is worth the effort and risk. Freelance thugs might work as muggers, stickup men, and armed robbers.
options.suggestedContacts: Other petty criminals, street-level law enforcement.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, or Intimidate)

options.label: Criminal: Gun Moll [Classic]
options.description: A female professional criminal. While many are fiercely independent, some are at the beck and call of mobster boyfriends—however, in reality this could easily be the other way around, with the moll working her boyfriend for all she can before skedaddling with all the cash and furs she can carry.
options.suggestedContacts: Gangsters, law enforcement, local businesses.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Criminal: Fence
options.description: A fence is someone who trades in stolen property, usually taking in stolen goods and then selling them to other criminals or (unwitting) legitimate customers. Principally a middleman between thieves and buyers.
options.suggestedContacts: Organized crime, trade contacts, black market and legitimate buyers.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Criminal: Forger/Counterfeiter
options.description: Forgers are the artists of the criminal world, specializing in forging official documents, deeds and transfers, and providing phony signatures. The best engrave plates for counterfeiting currency.
options.suggestedContacts: Organized crime, businesspeople.

options.label: Criminal: Smuggler
options.description: Smuggling is always a lucrative and high-risk business. The smuggler usually has a public profession, such as boat captain, pilot, or businessman, allowing them to transport illicit products to a needy market.
options.suggestedContacts: Organized crime, Coast Guard, U.S. Customs officials.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Drive Auto or Pilot (Aircraft or Boat)

options.label: Criminal: Street Punk
options.description: Street punks are typically young street hoods, possibly looking for a chance to hook-up with real gangsters. Experience is probably limited to stealing cars, shoplifting, mugging, and burglary.
options.suggestedContacts: Petty criminals, other punks, the local fence, maybe the local gangster, certainly the local police.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Cult Leader
options.description: America has always generated new religions. The leader is either a firm believer in the dogma they impart to the cult's members or simply in it for the money and power.
options.suggestedContacts: While the majority of followers will be regular people, the more charismatic the leader, the greater the possibility of celebrity followers, such as movie stars and rich widows.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Deprogrammer [Modern]
options.description: Deprogramming is the act of persuading (or forcing) a person to abandon their belief or allegiance to a religious or social community. Normally the deprogrammer is hired by relatives of an individual who has joined some form of cult.
options.suggestedContacts: Local and federal law enforcement, criminals, religious community.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Designer
options.description: Designers work in many fields, from fashion to furniture and most points in-between. The designer may work freelance, for a design house or for a business designing consumer products, processes, laws, games, graphics, and so on.
options.suggestedContacts: Advertising, media, furnishings, architectural, other.

options.label: Dilettante [Lovecraftian]
options.description: Dilettantes are self-supporting, living off an inheritance, trust fund, or some other source of income that does not require them to work. Probably well educated, though not necessarily accomplished in anything. Money frees the dilettante to be eccentric and outspoken.
options.suggestedContacts: Variable, but usually people of a similar background and tastes, fraternal organizations, bohemian circles, high society at large.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Diver
options.description: Divers could work in the military, law enforcement, or in civilian occupations like sponge gathering, salvage, conservation, or even treasure hunting.
options.suggestedContacts: Coast guard, ship captains, military, law enforcement, smugglers.

options.label: Doctor of Medicine [Lovecraftian]
options.description: Most likely a general practitioner, surgeon, or other specialist, such as a psychiatrist or an independent medical researcher. Apart from personal goals, three aims—helping patients, gaining money and prestige, and promoting a more rational and wiser society—are common to the profession.
options.suggestedContacts: Other physicians, medical workers, patients and ex-patients.

options.label: Drifter
options.description: As opposed to someone who is poverty-stricken, the drifter's wandering life is chosen, perhaps compensating for a social, philosophical, or economic lack, or perhaps due to a desire to break free of societal constraints. The drifter takes jobs, sometimes for days or months.
options.suggestedContacts: Other hobos, a few friendly railroad guards, soft touches in numerous towns.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Driver: Chauffeur
options.description: A chauffeur is either directly employed by an individual or firm, or works for an agency that hires both car and chauffeur out for single engagements or on a retainer basis.
options.suggestedContacts: Successful business people (criminals included), political representatives.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Driver
options.description: Professional drivers may work for a company, private individual, or possibly have their own cab or rig.
options.suggestedContacts: Customers, businesses, law enforcement and general street level life.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Taxi Driver
options.description: Taxi drivers may work for large or small companies, or possibly have their own cab and license. Taxis are required to be fitted with approved meters, periodically checked by the city's taxi board.
options.suggestedContacts: Street scene, possibly a notable customer now and then.

options.label: Editor
options.description: Editors work assigning stories to reporters, writing newspaper and magazine editorials, dealing with crises, and meeting deadlines. Occasionally they edit. Large newspapers have many editors, including managing editors who are more involved with business operations than news.
options.suggestedContacts: News industry, local government, specialists (e.g. fashion designers, sports, business), publishers.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Elected Official
options.description: Popularly-elected officials enjoy prestige commensurate with their position. Small-town mayors and township supervisors find their influence extends little beyond their municipality's borders. The mayors of big cities are well paid, wielding more influence and power than the governor of their respective state.
options.suggestedContacts: Political operatives, government, news media, business, foreign governments, possibly organized crime.

options.label: Engineer
options.description: A specialist in mechanical or electrical devices, employed in a civilian business or in the military, but also including inventors. The engineer applies scientific knowledge, mathematics, and a liberal amount of ingenuity to design solutions for technical problems.
options.suggestedContacts: Business or military workers, local government, architects.

options.label: Entertainer
options.description: This occupation might include clown, singer, dancer, comedian, juggler, musician, or anyone else who earns a living in front of an audience. These people love to be seen, love to show what they do best, and love the consequent applause.
options.suggestedContacts: Vaudeville, theater, film industry, entertainment critics, organized crime, and television (for modern-day).
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Explorer [Classic]
options.description: In the early twentieth century there are still unknown areas of the world that some people can make a career of exploring. Scientific grants, private donations, and museum contracts, combined with newspaper, magazine, book, and film rights often generate enough money to support the adventurer.
options.suggestedContacts: Major libraries, universities, museums, wealthy patrons, other explorers, publishers, foreign government officials, local tribes people.

options.label: Farmer
options.description: An agricultural worker who might own the land on which they raise crops or livestock, or who is employed to do the same. Rigorous and demanding, the life of the farmer is suited to those who enjoy manual labor and outdoor activities.
options.suggestedContacts: Local bank, local politicians, state agricultural department.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Federal Agent
options.description: There are a vast variety of federal law enforcement agencies and agents. Some are uniformed, such as the U.S. Marshals, while others, like the Federal Bureau of Investigation, are plainclothes and operate similar to detectives.
options.suggestedContacts: Federal agencies, law enforcement, organized crime.

options.label: Firefighter
options.description: Firefighters are civil servants, employed by the communities they serve. They work around the clock, on shifts lasting several days, eating, sleeping, and entertaining themselves within the confines of the fire station. Organized along military lines, promotion through lieutenant, captain, and chief is possible.
options.suggestedContacts: Civic workers, medical workers, law enforcement.

options.label: Foreign Correspondent
options.description: Foreign correspondents are the elite of news reporters. They work on salary, enjoy expense accounts, and travel the globe. Natural disasters, political upheaval, and war are the primary focus of the foreign correspondent.
options.suggestedContacts: National or worldwide news industry, foreign governments, military.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Forensic Surgeon
options.description: A highly specialized occupation, most forensic surgeons are employed by a city, county, or state to conduct autopsies, determine causes of death, and make recommendations to the prosecutor's office. Forensic surgeons are often called to give testimony at criminal proceedings.
options.suggestedContacts: Laboratories, law enforcement, medical profession.

options.label: Gambler
options.description: Gamblers are the dandies of the criminal world. Sharp dressers, they usually possess bags of charm, either earthy or sophisticated. Whether frequenting the racetrack, card table, or casino, such individuals often base their life upon chance.
options.suggestedContacts: Bookies, organized crime, street scene.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Gangster: Boss
options.description: The gangster boss oversees the business, makes deals, and determines how to solve problems. For the most part, the gangster boss is untouchable, able to field a small army of underlings to do his or her bidding. Gangsterism rose to prominence in the 1920s.
options.suggestedContacts: Organized crime, street-level crime, police, city government, politicians, judges, unions, lawyers, businesses, and residents of the same ethnic community.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Gangster: Underling
options.description: Underlings usually have specific areas of responsibility, such as overseeing illicit shipments, collecting protection money, and so on.
options.suggestedContacts: Street-level crime, police, businesses and residents of the same ethnic community.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Gentleman/Lady
options.description: A man or woman of good breeding, courteous behavior, and good conduct. Usually the term is applied to a member of the upper class who is independently wealthy (either by inheritance or regular allowance).
options.suggestedContacts: Upper classes and landed gentry, politics, servants and agricultural workers.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Hobo
options.description: Riding the rails continually, on the move from one town to another, they are penniless poets and vagabonds—explorers of the road, adventurers, and thieves. Life on the road or rails is dangerous.
options.suggestedContacts: Other hobos, a few friendly railroad guards, soft touches in numerous towns.

options.label: Hospital Orderly
options.description: The typical hospital orderly is in charge of emptying waste, cleaning rooms, taxiing patients, and any other odd job requiring slightly more skill than that of a janitor.
options.suggestedContacts: Hospital and medical workers, patients. Access to drugs, medical records, etc.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Journalist: Investigative Journalist
options.description: Uses words to report and comment upon topics and incidents of the day. The best investigative journalists report, but keep themselves independent of the corruption and self-serving they witness.
options.suggestedContacts: News industry, politicians, street-level crime or law enforcement.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Journalist: Reporter
options.description: Reporters work in the news and media industries, either freelance or for a newspaper, magazine, website, or news agency. Most work outside of the office, interviewing witnesses, checking records, and gathering stories.
options.suggestedContacts: News and media industries, political organizations and government, business, law enforcement, street criminals, high society.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Judge
options.description: An official who presides over court legal proceedings, either alone or within a group of peers. Judges are either appointed or elected, sometimes for a term of specified length, other times for life. Most judges are licensed attorneys.
options.suggestedContacts: Legal connections, possibly organized crime.

options.label: Laboratory Assistant
options.description: Working in a scientific environment, the assistant (or technician) performs laboratory and administrative tasks under the supervision of a lead scientist. Tasks may include sampling, testing, recording and analyzing results, and setting up or performing experiments.
options.suggestedContacts: Universities, scientists, librarians.

options.label: Laborer: Unskilled
options.description: Laborers include factory workers, mill hands, stevedores, road crews, miners, construction, and so on. While technically unskilled, the average laborer is often an expert in the use of power tools, lifting gear, and other equipment to be found on site.
options.suggestedContacts: Other workers and supervisors within their industry.

options.label: Laborer: Lumberjack
options.description: A lumberjack works in the forestry industry, felling trees and processing timber. The work is physically demanding and potentially dangerous.
options.suggestedContacts: Forestry workers, wilderness guides and conservationists.
skills: Natural World or Science (Biology or Botany)

options.label: Laborer: Miner
options.description: Mining is physically demanding and dangerous work. Miners extract coal, ore, and other minerals from the earth.
options.suggestedContacts: Union officials, political organizations.

options.label: Lawyer
options.description: Learned in the law of the state in which they serve, the lawyer acts as an attorney, counsel, or solicitor, able to relate abstract legal theories and knowledge to present solutions for their clients. With the right client, a lawyer could become a celebrity in his or her own right.
options.suggestedContacts: Organized crime, financiers, district attorneys and judges.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Librarian [Lovecraftian]
options.description: Librarians are most often employed by public institutions or universities, responsible for cataloging and maintaining stock, as well as dealing with lender enquiries. Some large businesses might employ a librarian to manage a large collection.
options.suggestedContacts: Booksellers, community groups, specialist researchers.

options.label: Mechanic and Skilled Trades
options.description: This occupation includes all types of trades requiring specialized training, time on the job as an apprentice or trainee, etc. Carpenters, stonemasons, plumbers, electricians, millwrights, mechanics, and others all qualify as skilled trades.
options.suggestedContacts: Union members, trade-relevant specialists.

options.label: Military Officer
options.description: Officers are command rank and most commissions demand some form of higher education. All armed services have established officer training programs. Upon graduation the cadet is promoted to the rank of Army or Marine Second Lieutenant or Naval Ensign.
options.suggestedContacts: Military, federal government.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Missionary
options.description: Missionaries visit remote corners of the globe dispensing the word of God. May be Catholic, Protestant, Islamic, or from another branch of faith. Sometimes a missionary may be independent of all except his or her own vision.
options.suggestedContacts: Church hierarchy, foreign officials.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Mountain Climber
options.description: Mountain climbing as a sport became popular in the 19th century. Most climbers are weekend and vacation sportsmen and women; only a few have the reputations needed to attract the financing and sponsorship required for major climbs.
options.suggestedContacts: Other climbers, environmentalists, patrons, sponsors, local rescue or law enforcement, park rangers, sports clubs.

options.label: Museum Curator
options.description: A museum curator can be responsible for a large facility like a university or other publicly funded institution, or any sort of smaller museum, often specializing in local geology or other such topics.
options.suggestedContacts: Local universities and scholars, publishers, museum patrons.

options.label: Musician
options.description: May perform in an orchestra, group, or solo, with any instrument you care to think of. Getting noticed is hard and then getting a recording contract is difficult. The 1920s is, of course, the Jazz Age, and musicians work in small combos and dance orchestras in large and medium-sized cities and towns across America.
options.suggestedContacts: Club owners, musicians' union, organized crime, street-level criminals.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Nurse
options.description: Nurses are trained healthcare assistants, usually working in hospitals, nursing homes, or with General Practitioners. Generally, a nurse will assist an individual, sick or well, with activities contributing to health or recovery.
options.suggestedContacts: Hospital workers, physicians, community workers.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Occultist
options.description: Occultists are students of esoteric secrets and arcane magic. They fully believe in paranormal abilities and actively attempt to learn about and discover these powers within themselves. Most are familiar with a broad range of different philosophies and magical theories.
options.suggestedContacts: Libraries, occult societies or fraternities, other occultists.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Outdoorsman/woman
options.description: The outdoor enthusiast spends much of his or her time living in the wilderness, often alone for long periods. Commonly skilled in hunting and fishing, and able to be self-sufficient in all but the harshest of environments.
options.suggestedContacts: Local people and native folk, traders.

options.label: Parapsychologist
options.description: Parapsychologists do not pretend to enjoy extraordinary powers, but instead spend their efforts attempting to observe, record, and study such instances. Sometimes nicknamed ghost hunters, they make use of technology to try to capture hard evidence of paranormal activity.
options.suggestedContacts: Universities, parapsychological publications.

options.label: Pharmacist
options.description: Pharmacists have long been more closely regulated than physicians. They are licensed by individual states, most of them requiring a high school education and three years of pharmacy school. A pharmacist may be employed in a hospital or a drug store, or perhaps they own a dispensary.
options.suggestedContacts: Local community, local physicians, hospitals and patients. Access to all manner of chemicals and drugs.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Photographer
options.description: Photography as an art form has been around a long time, with most photographers working freelance, for an advertising firm, or in a portrait studio. Others are employed in the newspaper, media, and film industries.
options.suggestedContacts: Advertising industry, local clients (including political organizations and newspapers).
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Photojournalist
options.description: Photojournalists are essentially reporters who use cameras, but who are also expected to write prose to accompany an image. In the 1920s, newsreels came into being; heavy, bulky 35mm film equipment was hauled around the globe in search of exciting news stories.
options.suggestedContacts: News industry, film industry (1920s), foreign governments and authorities.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Pilot
options.description: Professional aviators may be employed by business, for example the U.S. Mail, or work as pilots for a large or small commercial passenger line. In the U.S. there was no national regulation of pilots until the passing of the Air Commerce Act of 1926.
options.suggestedContacts: Old military contacts, cabin crew, mechanics, airfield staff, carnival entertainers.

options.label: Aviator [Classic]
options.description: An aviator is a stunt pilot, working at carnivals or offering daring leisure rides for those willing to pay. Often fame could be won by competing in organized air races. During the twenties, Hollywood makes fair use of stunt pilots.
options.suggestedContacts: Old military contacts, other pilots, airfield mechanics, businessmen.

options.label: Police Detective
options.description: The plainclothes branch of police agencies, detectives examine crime scenes, gather evidence, conduct interviews, and try to solve homicides, major burglaries, and other felonies. They work the streets, often in close cooperation with a uniformed patrol.
options.suggestedContacts: Law enforcement, street level crime, coroner's office, judiciary, organized crime.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Uniformed Police Officer
options.description: The uniformed police officer is employed by cities and towns, by County sheriffs' departments, and state or regional police forces. The job may be on foot, behind the wheel of a patrol car, or sitting at a desk.
options.suggestedContacts: Law enforcement, local businesses and residents, street level crime, organized crime.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Private Investigator
options.description: The private eye usually acts in non-police situations, gathering information and evidence for private clients in impending civil cases, tracking down fleeing spouses or business partners, or acting as an agent for private defense attorneys in criminal cases.
options.suggestedContacts: Law enforcement, clients.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Professor [Lovecraftian]
options.description: Professors are academics employed by colleges and universities. Larger corporations may also employ such academics for research and product development. The professor is qualified to teach and to perform competent research, and may have a discernible academic reputation in his or her area of expertise.
options.suggestedContacts: Scholars, universities, libraries.

options.label: Prospector
options.description: Though the days of the California Gold Rush and the Nevada Comstock Lode are long gone, the independent prospector is still a feature of the American West. Roaming the mountains, they endlessly search for the big strike that will make them rich. In these days, oil may be as good as gold.
options.suggestedContacts: Local businesses and residents.

options.label: Prostitute
options.description: Depending on circumstance, breeding, and background, a prostitute may be a high-rolling expensive call girl, male gigolo, or streetwalker. Often driven by circumstance, many dream of a way out.
options.suggestedContacts: Street scene, police, possibly organized crime, personal clientele.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Psychiatrist
options.description: In the modern era a psychiatrist is a physician specialized in the diagnosis and treatment of mental disorders. Psychiatrists are trained in psychopharmacological treatments and are authorized to prescribe psychiatric medication.
options.suggestedContacts: Others in the field of mental illness, physicians and possibly legal professions.

options.label: Psychologist/Psychoanalyst
options.description: While commonly known for the fields of psychotherapy and counseling, this is just one branch of psychology. Other specialists include organizational psychologists who work with businesses and governments, as well as academics conducting research or teaching psychology.
options.suggestedContacts: Psychological community, patients.

options.label: Researcher
options.description: The academic world sponsors a good deal of research, particularly in the fields of astronomy, physics, and other theoretical sciences. Field researchers are usually highly experienced, independent, and resourceful, possibly employed by private interests or undertaking academic research for a university.
options.suggestedContacts: Scholars, and academics, large businesses and corporations, foreign governments and individuals.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Sailor: Naval
options.description: Naval sailors go through an initial period of basic training. Upon graduation they are assigned rates and stations. The Navy also has a need for technically trained mechanics, radio operators, air controllers, etc. The highest enlisted rank is Chief Petty Officer.
options.suggestedContacts: Military, veterans' associations.

options.label: Sailor: Commercial
options.description: Commercial sailors may work aboard a fishing vessel, charter boat, or haulage tankers, carrying oil or commodities. Charter boats work both coasts, as well as on the Great Lakes, catering to sport fisherman and vacationers.
options.suggestedContacts: Coast Guard, smugglers, organized crime.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Salesperson
options.description: An integral part of many businesses, salespeople work to promote and sell their employer's goods or services. Most spend much of their time traveling, meeting, and entertaining clients. Some are mainly office-based, while others travel between communities to sell their wares door-to-door.
options.suggestedContacts: Businesses within the same sector, favored customers.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Scientist
options.description: Natural philosophers who delve reality in the pursuit of knowledge. Where science is applied to creating useful items, one might employ an engineer, but if one wishes to expand the bounds of what is possible, one will require scientists.
options.suggestedContacts: Other scientists and academics, universities, their employers and former employers.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Secretary
options.description: A position that ranges from highly-paid private executive assistants to those in the typing pool. The work concerns supporting executives and managers with a range of communication and organizational skills. Being at the hub of the business, most secretaries know more about the inner workings and operations of the business than their bosses do.
options.suggestedContacts: Other office workers, senior executives in client firms.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Shopkeeper
options.description: The owner of a small shop, market stall, or perhaps restaurant. Usually self-employed, but may be the manager running the shop on behalf of the owner. Possibly family-run, with relations also working on the premises.
options.suggestedContacts: Local residents and businesses, local police, local government, customers.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Soldier/Marine
options.description: This refers to the enlisted ranks of the Army and Marines and includes the lowest ranks of Private up through Gunnery Sergeant. All enlisted persons receive basic training—boot camp—where raw recruits are taught how to march, shoot, and salute.
options.suggestedContacts: Military, veterans associations.
skills: First Aid or Mechanical Repair or Other Language
First Aid or Mechanical Repair or Other Language

options.label: Spy
options.description: Spies work for the intelligence community of a political state or organization. They may appear in any form of occupation, from ambassador to kitchen cleaner, in order to obtain the information they require. Some work deep cover for many years.
options.suggestedContacts: Generally only the person the spy reports to, possibly other connections developed while under cover.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Student/Intern
options.description: May be a student enrolled at a college or university, or the employee of a company receiving minimal compensation for valuable on-the-job training.
options.suggestedContacts: Academics and other students, while interns may also know business people.

options.label: Stuntman
options.description: The film and television industry employs stuntmen and women to simulate falls from buildings, car crashes, and other catastrophes. Stunt performers will usually be trained in fighting techniques and stage combat. There is always risk in the performance of all stunt work, and health and safety is a key element.
options.suggestedContacts: The film and television industries, various explosive and pyrotechnic firms, actors and directors.
skills: Diving or Drive Automobile or Pilot (any) or Ride

options.label: Tribe Member
options.description: In the sense of family allegiance, at least, tribalism is everywhere. In a tribe, the primacy of kinship and custom is self-evident. A tribal group is nominally relatively small. In place of a blanket of law and general individual rights, the tribal personality defers to personal honor.
options.suggestedContacts: Fellow tribe members.

options.label: Undertaker
options.description: Undertakers, also known as morticians or funeral directors, manage the business of funeral rites. This work includes the burial or cremation of the dead. With burials, the undertaker will perform embalming, as well as dressing, casketing, and cosmetizing the appearance of the deceased.
options.suggestedContacts: Few.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Union Activist
options.description: Union activists are organizers, leaders, and sometimes either visionaries or malcontents with an axe to grind. Normally a friend to the workers and an enemy of the bosses. Unions exist in all walk of life, be it stevedores, construction workers, miners, or actors.
options.suggestedContacts: Other labor leaders and activists, political friends, possibly organized crime. In the 1920s, also socialists, communists, and subversive anarchists.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Waitress/Waiter
options.description: Serves customers and clients within a hotel, bar, or other drinking or eating establishment. Traditionally low-paid, the waitress earns tips from clients by providing good service and trying to establish a rapport.
options.suggestedContacts: Customers, organized crime.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: White-Collar Worker: Clerk/Executive
options.description: The lowest-level white-collar position of a clerk to a middle or senior manager. Clerks are habitually underpaid and the work is drudgery, with those recognized as having talent being earmarked for promotion someday.
options.suggestedContacts: Other office workers.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: White-Collar Worker: Middle/Senior Manager
options.description: Middle and senior managers attract higher salaries, with greater responsibilities and say in how the business is managed day-to-day. Executive types are often family-oriented, with a spouse at home and children.
options.suggestedContacts: Old college connections, Masons or other fraternal groups, local and federal government, media and marketing.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Zealot
options.description: Intense and vision-driven, scorning an easy life, the zealot agitates for a better life for humanity, or for some advantage for the group imagined to be the most worthwhile part of humanity. Some fanatics promote their beliefs through violence; however, the peaceable majority may be just as implacable.
options.suggestedContacts: Religious or fraternal groups, news media.
skills: Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)
Interpersonal skill (Charm, Fast Talk, Intimidate, or Persuade)

options.label: Zookeeper
options.description: Zookeepers are responsible for the feeding and care of the animals in their charge; groundskeepers and attendants take care of other chores. Often the zookeeper is specialized in a particular breed of animal. The Zookeeper is able to use the Medicine skill on animals.
options.suggestedContacts: Scientists, environmentalists.

children.name: characteristics.Strength
children.label: Strength
children.description: Strength measures the muscle power of an investigator. The higher it is, the more the investigator can lift or tightly cling to something. This characteristic determines the damage an investigator inflicts in hand-to-hand combat. Reduced to STR 0, an investigator is an invalid, unable to get out of bed

children.name: characteristics.Constitution
children.label: Constitution
children.description: Constitution represents health, vigor, and vitality. Poisons and diseases may directly challenge an investigator's constitution.Investigators with a high constitution often have more hit points—the better to resist injury and attack. Serious physical injury or magical attack might lower the statistic, and if constitution reaches zero, the investigator dies.

children.name: characteristics.Size
children.label: Size
children.description: Size averages both height and weight into a single number. To see over a wall, to squeeze through a small opening, or even to judge whose head might be sticking up out of the grass, use size. Size helps determine hit points and damage bonus and build. One might decrease SIZ to indicate the loss of a limb, though lowering DEX is more often the solution. Presumably if investigators lose all SIZ points they disappear—goodness knows to where!

children.name: characteristics.Dexterity
children.label: Dexterity
children.description: Investigators with higher Dexterity scores are quicker, nimbler and more physically flexible. A DEX roll might be made to grab a support to keep from falling, to move faster than an opponent, or to accomplish some delicate task. An investigator with zero DEX is uncoordinated and unable to perform physical tasks. In combat, the character with the highest DEX acts first.

children.name: characteristics.Appearance
children.label: Appearance
children.description: Appearance measures both physical attractiveness and personality. A person with high APP is charming and likeable, but may lack conventional good looks. Someone with APP of 0 is appallingly ugly, possibly with a wholly detestable demeanor, provoking comment and shock everywhere. APP may be useful in social encounters or when trying to make a good impression.

children.name: characteristics.Intelligence
children.label: Intelligence
children.description: Intelligence represents how well investigators learn, remember, analyze information and solve complex puzzles. An investigator with zero INT is a babbling, drooling idiot. Intelligence determines the number of Personal Interest skill points (multiply INT x 2) allotted to a new investigator (see Personal Interests page 36). INT also acts as the value for both Idea rolls and Intelligence rolls (see Chapter 5: Game System). If the amount of INT seems to contradict another characteristic, that's another chance for roleplaying and further defining your investigator. For example, an investigator with high EDU and low INT might be a pedantic teacher or a sideshow performer, someone who knows facts but not their meanings. Conversely, high INT and low EDU might mean ignorance—such as an uneducated farm boy, new to the Big City—however this person would not be dull-witted.

children.name: characteristics.Power
children.label: Power
children.description: Power indicates force of will; the higher the POW, the higher the aptitude for, and resistance to, magic. An investigator with zero POW is zombie-like and without 'purpose,' as well as being unable to use magic. Unless stated otherwise, POW that is lost during the game is lost permanently. Sanity points (SAN) begin the game equal to the character's POW. Sanity points are covered in more detail in Chapter 8: Sanity. POW dictates the character's number of 'magic points,' which, unlike POW, can be spent and regenerated during play. Magic points are equal to one-fifth of POW (see Chapter 10: Magic). The POW of ordinary characters and investigators rarely changes. However, those adroit in the mysteries of the magic of the Cthulhu Mythos may be able to increase their POW.

children.name: characteristics.Education
children.label: Education
children.description: Education is a measure of the formal and factual knowledge possessed by the investigator, as well as indicating the time the investigator has spent in full-time education. EDU measures retained information, not the intelligent application of that information (see Intelligence, above). An investigator without EDU would be like a newborn baby or an amnesiac—without knowledge of the world, probably very curious and credulous. An EDU of 60 suggests the investigator is a high school graduate, while a score of around 70 indicates a person with some college years. Those with an EDU greater than 80 have most likely conducted graduate level work and have a degree, as expected of a person who has been to a university of some kind. Note that sometimes a person with a high Education may not necessarily be schooled in the traditional sense, but rather may be self-taught and possess a highly studious and observant nature. EDU is a factor in determining how many Occupational skill points (see Occupation Skills page 36) an investigator begins with, and represents the investigator's starting percentage for the Own Language skill (see page 67). EDU is also used when making Know rolls (see Chapter 5: Game System)

children.name: characteristics.Luck
children.label: Luck
children.description: Luck rolls may be called for by the Keeper when circumstances external to any investigator are in question, and also when determining the fickle hand of fate. If a skill or characteristic is applicable to a situation then it should be used rather than Luck. If the Keeper calls for a Group Luck roll, the player whose investigator has the lowest Luck score (among those present in the scene) should make the roll. If something bad is deemed to happen to one member of the group, the Keeper can simply ask who has the lowest Luck score at that moment and have that individual suffer the unfortunate event.

options.label: Amaxophobia
options.description: Fear of being in or riding in vehicles.

options.label: Anglophobia
options.description: Fear of England or English culture, etc.

options.label: Gerontophobia
options.description: Fear of old people or of growing old.

options.label: Hypnophobia
options.description: Fear of sleep or of being hypnotized.

options.label: Merinthophobia
options.description: Fear of being bound or tied up.

options.label: Onomatophobia
options.description: Fear of hearing a certain word or words.

options.label: Phagophobia
options.description: Fear of swallowing, of eating or of being eaten.

options.label: Potophobia
options.description: Fear of alcohol or alcoholic beverages.

options.label: Scotophobia
options.description: Fear of darkness or of the night.

options.label: Stenophobia
options.description: Fear of narrow things or places.

options.label: Taphephobia
options.description: Fear of being buried alive or of cemeteries.

options.label: Xanthophobia
options.description: Fear of the color yellow or the word "yellow".

options.label: Agromania
options.description: Intense desire to be in open spaces.

options.label: Aichmomania
options.description: Obsession with sharp or pointed objects.

options.label: Cacodemomania
options.description: Pathological belief that one is inhabited by an evil spirit.

options.label: Cartacoethes
options.description: Uncontrollable compulsion to see maps everywhere.

options.label: Catapedamania
options.description: Obsession with jumping from high places.

options.label: Cheimatomania
options.description: Abnormal desire for cold and/or cold things.

options.label: Clinomania
options.description: Excessive desire to stay in bed.

options.label: Demonomania
options.description: Pathological belief that one is possessed by demons.

options.label: Dermatillomania
options.description: Compulsion for picking at one's skin.

options.label: Enosimania
options.description: Pathological belief that one has sinned.

options.label: Gamomania
options.description: Obsession with issuing odd marriage proposals.

options.label: Habromania
options.description: Abnormal tendency to create pleasant delusions (in spite of reality).

options.label: Idolomania
options.description: Obsession or devotion to an idol.

options.label: Ligyromania
options.description: Uncontrollable compulsion to make loud or shrill noises.

options.label: Lotterymania
options.description: An extreme desire to take part in lotteries.

options.label: Lypemania
options.description: An abnormal tendency toward deep melancholy.

options.label: Megalithomania
options.description: Abnormal tendency to compose bizarre ideas when in the presence of stone circles/standing stones.

options.label: Melomania
options.description: Obsession with music or a specific tune.

options.label: Misomania
options.description: Hatred of everything, obsession of hating some subject or group.

options.label: Monomania
options.description: Abnormal obsession with a single thought or idea.

options.label: Mythomania
options.description: Lying or exaggerating to an abnormal extent.

options.label: Nosomania
options.description: Delusion of suffering from an imagined disease.

options.label: Onomatomania
options.description: Irresistible desire to repeat certain words.

options.label: Opsomania
options.description: Abnormal love for one kind of food.

options.label: Planomania
options.description: Abnormal desire to disobey social norms.

options.label: Siderodromoania
options.description: Intense fascination with trains and railroad travel.

options.label: Sophomania
options.description: The delusion that one is incredibly intelligent.

options.label: Thanatomania
options.description: Belief that one is cursed by death magic.

options.label: Theomania
options.description: Belief that he or she is a god.

options.label: Trichotillomania
options.description: Craving for pulling out own hair.

options.label: Apportion Ka
options.description:
A portion of the caster's life essence, or "ka," is transferred into one of his or her vital organs. The enchanted organ is then removed from the caster's body, to be hidden away for safekeeping and providing the wizard with a form of invulnerability—as long as the enchanted organ remains safely hidden the wizard will not suffer particular attacks and may even be invulnerable to death.
This spell was first used by followers of the Dark Pharaoh, Nephren-Ka, who would remove a vital organ, such as the heart or liver, and lock them away in safe places. This would make the caster virtually impossible to kill, if not for the spell's one weakness: the brain is the seat of the spell's power and could not be removed. If the brain was destroyed, the other organs would lose their magical properties and the caster would die.
Having one's own insides removed costs 2D10 Sanity points and the permanent loss of 5 POW per organ removed. Attacks that impale cause only normal damage unless the head is specifically targeted. If the caster is harmed, hit points should be reduced as usual, leading to unconsciousness but not death.
options.cost: 10 magic points, 5 POW and 2D10 Sanity points per organ

options.label: Banishment of Yde Etad
options.description:
This spell sends home those troublesome human or human-like intelligences that are under their own volition. It is not effective against any creature commanded by another intelligence. If correctly performed, the banishment is permanent and irrevocable. Each banishment is of an individual target, not of a type of monsters.
At least three people must participate in the spell. All participants must know the spell. More participants add to the effectiveness; however, the total number of participants must be divisible by three.
This banishment works by destroying with ritual flaming and salt the target's persona. The target's personal sigil is best, but a substitute can be prepared according to a formula described within the spell. For the spell to take effect a 21-hit point wound must be made between the caster with the highest POW and target. One bonus die is granted for each additional set of three casters.
options.cost: 1D4+3 magic points per caster; 1D4 Sanity points each

options.label: Bless Blade
options.description: Creates a blade capable of damaging or killing entities that cannot be harmed by mundane weapons. Requires the blood sacrifice of an animal of at least SIZ 50. The blade of the knife must be of an elemental metal such as iron or silver. The blade may be of any size; however, larger blades do greater damage. If the blade is broken, melted, or otherwise damaged it permanently loses this ability; however, it will not be effective again in supernormal circumstances.

options.label: Body Warping of Gorgoroth
options.description: Allows the caster to change his or her physical form. The sorcerer can change body shape and appearance, though retaining personal abilities. The form is flexible but it can appear to be made of stone, wood, a rag, etc. Once changed into a new shape, the caster has the mobility and mass of that shape. The caster's STR, CON, INT, POW and DEX do not change. If emulating a person, the sorcerer's appearance becomes that of the individual simulated. The caster can take on only a known form.

options.label: Breath of the Deep
options.description: The target's lungs fill with seawater, potentially causing an unpleasant death by drowning. The caster must be able to see the target. After mentally intoning the spell for a round for the spell to take effect the caster must win an opposed POW roll with the target. If the caster wins the target begins to drown—the target falls to the ground, taking 2D10 damage and taking 1D6 damage each round. The target should make an Extreme CON roll (equal to or below one-fifth CON) after taking damage each round. If the roll is successful the water has been expelled and the effects of the spell cease.

options.label: Brew Space-Mead
options.description: A wonderfully golden liquid, which the kept in a carafe in his desk and served in tiny Belgian liqueur glasses in such small amounts that it seemed futile even to taste it to one's lips. This spell creates a magical drink that allows a human to withstand journeys through the vacuum of space. Brewing space-mead requires five special ingredients to be chosen by the Keeper, requires at least a week of brewing time. Once the mead is foaming and bubbling, the caster must sacrifice 20 magic points per dose into the brew. These magic points must be sacrificed over many days; the more points, the more doses. Each dose allows one separate stage for the caster/user. Different types of space-mead exist, each with the same effects but all of them requiring different ingredients.
options.cost: 20 magic points per dose; plus variable additional magic and Sanity points

options.label: Call Deity
options.description:
Call spells bring the avatar, god, or Great Old One before the caster. Call and Dismiss spells are listed here to exist for even cultists. Only cult priests or desperate people should choose to call a Mythos deity. A handful are cited here. Both Call and Dismiss spells are likely to exist for every Great Old One and for every form of Mythos god.
A caster can assist Call spells by contributing 1 magic point, with the caster acting as the focus for the group. Everyone present expends 1 magic point. Those who know the spell can sacrifice a number of magic points (including burning hit points). The total of the magic points spent is the percentage chance that the spell works. For each magic point spent, the group must chant for a minute, but never for more than 100 minutes.
options.cost: 1 magic point per person; 1D10 Sanity points (caster only)

options.label: Call Azathoth
options.description: Calling this god is extremely dangerous and has the potential to destroy the entire caster. The spell can be cast only outside at night; however, no other special preparations are required. Once on Earth, Azathoth is insane and has no motivations—casters make contact at their peril. This is distinctly different to Summoning Spells that have the potential to allow casters to bind the summoned beings to their will.
options.cost: Variable magic points; 1D10 Sanity points (caster only)

options.label: Call Cthugha
options.description: Cthugha invites being through a flame held by the caster (a candle, torch, etc.). As the caster intones the spell, he or she moves the flame in series of patterns. The spell can successfully cast only on clear nights when Fomalhaut (the brightest star in the Piscis Austrinus constellation) clears the horizon, September to November are the best nights for Fomalhaut in the Northern Hemisphere.
options.cost: Variable magic points; 1D10 Sanity points (caster only)

options.label: Call Hastur
options.description: Calls the deity to raise large blocks of stone arranged in a V pattern (always a sign of cult activity!). Each of the stone blocks needs a white or tan stone at least one cubic yard. The spell can be cast only on clear nights when Aldebaran (in the constellation of Taurus) is above the horizon, roughly October through February. Each bushel spent on Call Hastur adds a further 10 percentiles to the chance for success. If 5 points of POW have been sacrificed into each of the stone blocks, the spell becomes V'adds a further 30 percentiles to the chance to cast Summon/Bind Byakhee.
options.cost: Variable magic points; 1D10 Sanity points (caster only)

options.label: Call Ithaqua
options.description: Focuses the attention of Ithaqua; however, the presence of the Great Old One may be noticed only as a whirlwind or strong icy wind. The spell must be cast in an enormous mountain, high-altitude location. Some wizards state that the spell may only be performed in the far north of the world, when temperatures are below freezing. Some wizards conjecture that Ithaqua might be invoked from any high, snowy mountain, even those in the South Pole.
options.cost: Variable magic points; 1D10 Sanity points (caster only)

options.label: Call Nyogtha
options.description: Invokes the favor of Nyogtha, who appears to grant whatever the caster does if Nyogtha deems him worthy. The caster must perform the spell ritual at the entrance to any cavern (certain tomes hint that all great caverns lead to the same place where Nyogtha dwells).
options.cost: Variable magic points; 1D10 Sanity points (caster only)

options.label: Call Shah-Neklah
options.description: Calls Shah-Neklah—Nigguraths in a consecrated stone altar. This alter does not perform the sacrifice—the hunting horror serves the sacrifice. The caster must step away from the altar, speaking the spell backward, to bind the Horror.
options.cost: Variable magic points; 1D10 Sanity points (caster only)

options.label: Call Yog-Sothoth
options.description: Calls Yog-Sothoth to a specially constructed stone tower. The tower must be in an open area and the sky must be cloudless. The stone tower must be at least 30 feet high. Each cult has its own protocol; one must designate a human sacrifice for the god's taking. This need be no more than an invitation—gesturing toward a nearby person, for instance, from which Yog-Sothoth then selects a victim. At the cult's option, the stone tower may be enchanted, lowering the magic points needed for a successful cast of the spell.
options.cost: Variable magic points; 1D10 Sanity points (caster only)

options.label: Dismiss Deity
options.description: Every Dismiss spell differs: the caster must know the specific Dismiss spell for the particular deity. First, if no POW (rounded down) possessed by the deity is donated to the spell by a cultist, this grants an initial 5% chance to dismiss the god, and it opens the way for the deity's dismissal. The way once prepared, the sacrifice of more magic points can tempt the deity into departure. In this, the Dismiss spell is the reverse of the Call spell, though particular conditions or requirements may be unique.
options.cost: 1 minute plus 1 extra round per participant who donates magic points

options.label: Cause/Cure Blindness
options.description: The target suffers complete and permanent blindness, as if by a stroke. The same spell can reverse blindness if the eyes are not physically destroyed. The spell requires 8 magic points for either version. A day of ritual is required. For the spell to take effect the caster must succeed in an opposed POW roll with the target (automatic success if the target is consenting).
options.cost: 8 magic points; 1D6 Sanity points for Cause Blindness

options.label: Chant of Theth
options.description: The use of this spell increases the caster's mental faculties. Working his or her way to a string of particular intellectual problem. Spending 10 magic points in the casting enables the user to increase his or her INT roll to gain knowledge, learn a spell, translate a passage, deduce the meaning of a symbol, etc.

options.label: Cloud Memory
options.description:
The spell blocks the target's ability to consciously remember a particular event. The caster must be able to see the target and the target must be able to receive the caster's suggestion for this spell to have effect. Immediately if the caster succeeds in an opposed POW roll with the target it wins; the target's mind is mentally blocked regarding one specified incident. If the incident was terrifying, the victim may thereafter still have nightmares vaguely relating to it.
The caster cannot undo the spell from the target's mind—he or she must cite a certain event, such as "Forget you were assaulted by a monster." This spell cannot block knowledge of spells or of the Cthulhu Mythos unless the knowledge is firmly tied to a specific event, nor can it undo a Sanity loss or undo unconsciousness.

options.label: Clutch of Nyogtha
options.description:
The caster must spend 1 magic point to begin the spell and the target must be within conversational distance. For the spell to take effect the caster must succeed in an opposed POW roll with the target. If the caster wins the roll, the target feels as though an arm, hand or tentacle is crushing his or her heart, losing 1D3 hit points for each combat round that the spell is in effect. While under such attack, the target is constantly paralyzed, as though having a heart attack. In the round that the accumulated damage from this spell reduces his or her points to zero, the target's chest ruptures and bursts, and the target's smoking heart appears in the hand of the caster.
Around each round the spell lasts, the caster must spend magic points equal to twice the number of hit points in damage. The caster must concentrate on the spell each round that it is to remain in effect and the caster must defeat the target on an opposed POW roll each round. If the caster is distracted or if the target successfully resists, the spell ends. Any damage already done remains.
options.cost: 1+ magic points equal to twice the number of hit points in damage that round; 1D10 Sanity points

options.label: Consume Likewise
options.description: The caster can assume the living likeness of a person freshly dead, acquiring the victim's attributes, X-ray eyes, and so on. The caster assumes the being's living appearance, though which internal organs begin to replicate in the target. The experience of the spell is devastating; however, successful Psychoanalysis can help the victim come to terms with these applicable dreams and hallucinations, and restore half of each such Sanity loss.
options.cost: 10 magic points, every 6 hours of casting; 5 POW; 1D20 Sanity points

options.label: Contact Spells
options.description:
Using Contact spells a cultist could get in touch with monsters and alien races, perhaps to learn about magic, gods, or alien places. The caster should have a definite goal in mind; bargains might be struck and plans negotiated.
Contact spells do not give the caster the upper hand though; the spell is merely a method of establishing contact. Remember these are independent and intelligent beings with their own motivations—casters make contact at their peril. Knowing one Contact spell is of no help in casting another.
Cast properly this spell always works, unless there are no such things living within a convenient distance. If a contacted entity is large, such as a flying polyp, it may wish to swarm threatening, in order not to seem threatening. Once the contacted thing appears, it is free to depart, so if the caster has something to offer it, the chance for an extended meeting greatly improves.
options.cost: Relative to specific spell; Sanity cost: 1D3 (plus 1 or more to view the monster)

options.label: Contact Cthonian
options.description: Unless there are no chthonians nearby, it succeeds automatically. The spell must be cast at a location that has shown recent earthquakes or where hot spots and volcanoes offer relatively quick transport from the deep interior of the planet to the outer edge of the geologic crust.

options.label: Contact Deep One
options.description: Unless there are no deep ones nearby, the chanting succeeds automatically. This spell should be cast near the edge of a salt-water ocean. To work best, it should be cast near a deep one city, such as the waters off Innsmouth, Massachusetts or the coast near the town of Dunwich. Each version of the ritual insists on throwing specially inscribed stones into the water.

options.label: Contact Elder Thing
options.description: Unless there are no elder things nearby, the chanting succeeds automatically. The most likely locations are along the southern part of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge or over the geological trenches nearest Antarctica.

options.label: Contact Flying Polyp
options.description: Unless there are no flying polyps nearby, it succeeds automatically. Flying polyps have their rare underground cities, the largest of which is beneath the City in the Sands, in the western Australian desert. The caster should first construct or open a communication shell for their egress.

options.label: Contact Formless Spawn
options.description: Unless there are no formless spawn nearby, it succeeds automatically. The best place to cast is at a temple of Tsathoggua, whose altar statues of Tsathoggua is still present or at a spot near an opening into the earth with one of the following: a cave. The Oklahoma. Possibly other places in Western or Midwestern North America or caves northeast of points. One such cavern reportedly exists near Dunwich, Massachusetts.

options.label: Contact Ghoul
options.description: Unless there are no ghouls nearby, it succeeds automatically. Ghouls are found wherever large concentrations of humans are, especially near graveyards and crypts. Places of burial more than a century old are propitious locations for this spell. Moonlit nights are best.

options.label: Contact Gnaph-Keh
options.description: Unless there are no gnaph-keh nearby, it succeeds automatically. Gnaph-keh are usually found in Greenland or other frozen wastes near the North Pole. The spell must be augmented with a successful Sing (Art & Craft specialism) roll and before doing so the caster must build a small effigy of the beast from ice and snow.

options.label: Contact Hound of Tindalos
options.description: If a hound of Tindalos is summoned it will come automatically. There is no known way to argue with a hound of Tindalos, whose motive for moving through the stream of time and space is pure hunger.

options.label: Contact Mi-Go
options.description: Unless there are no mi-go nearby, it succeeds automatically. The spell must be cast at the base or the top of a high mountain—in a maze known to exist on the Plateau of Leng, for example. Among such ranges are the Appalachians, Andes and the Himalayas, and some peaks in Central Africa.

options.label: Contact Sand-Dweller
options.description: Unless there are no sand-dwellers nearby, it succeeds automatically. The spell must be cast in a suitably arid desert, such as in the Sahara, the southwestern United States, Saudi Arabia, or central Australia.

options.value: Contact Servitor of the Outer Gods
options.label: Contact Servitor of the Outer Gods
